text:
The discovery of radical cross-coupling reactions has revolutionized the field of organic synthesis, allowing access to thousands of structurally diverse molecules that were previously inaccessible by transition metal catalysis or photoredox methods. Except for the transition metal and photoredox platforms, almost all known cross-coupling protocols at the activites required for chemical synthesis have been single-event processes lacking a discernable activated intermediate that returns to a resting state following each catalytic cycle. Recent results with p-block elements such as bromine, aluminum and main group actinides have confirmed that some p-block elements can exhibit two-electron redox cycles analogous to those of late transition metals. Here, we demonstrate for the first time the ability of p-block elements to catalyze the formation of one-electron activated radical intermediates corresponding to first-row transition metals such as nickel and copper.

We report here for the first time on the activation of redox-active electrophiles via a bismuth-mediated radical oxidative addition. We used low valent bismuthidene for the single-electron transfer of alkyl phthalimide esters and Katritzky salts leading to stable Bi(III)-C(sp3) adducts. The adducts are formed in an equilibrium with their respective in-cage radical pairs undergoing homolytic bond cleavage and follow radical type reactivity like simple Giese additions.

Utilizing the reactivity, we also disclose the first example of a bismuth-catalyzed C(sp3)–N cross-coupling reaction. The catalyst engages the unusual reactivity of the α-amino alkyl moieties, which upon bismuth-induced oxidation lead to the formation of the electrophilic iminium ions. Notably, the reaction proceeds under highly sustainable, mild and catalyst-controlled, autonomous activatable catalyst (AAC) conditions, in the absence of any photoredox catalysts, any stoichiometric oxidants and any electrochemical setup and therefore is applicable for a wide range of unsubstituted and substituted NH-heterocycles and even to bioactive molecules. Our studies not only further expand the pnictogen redox chemistry but for the first time showcase the powerful applications of the bismuth radical catalysis in the modern day synthesis in organic chemistry.
explanation: High likelihood (~65%). The text uses highly polished prose with multiple 'for the first time' claims and some unusual phrasing that is common in AI-generated abstracts, though it could also be real chemistry research.
